title: What problems do Shinji and Rei have
display_title: What problems do Shinji and Rei have
wikitext:
[]: What problems do Shinji (and Rei) have?             Quote Post by ILUVASUKALANGLEY » Mon Sep 09, 2013 8:29 am Minor tweak to topic title to include the Rei tangent. Does anybody know what Anno has publicly stated what mental problems or disorders Shinji has like would Shinji be labeled as autistic or in the Autism Spectrum or Schizoeffective or schizoprenia or any of that nature? Sorry if its been asked before I've not been on here in a long time.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Sep 09, 2013 8:50 am Anno hasn't specifically said there's anything wrong with him other than being deeply depressed, though it is known that he was using some sort of abnormal psychology book as a reference. Some theorize that the children are modeled vaguelly on personality dissorers from different 'clusters'. Specifically, that Shinji is Avoidant, Asuka is Histrionic, and Rei is Schizotypal. Google those and decide for yourself if you think they fit.

[]:             Quote Post by SimplyMason0 » Mon Sep 09, 2013 10:18 am I don't think Rei can be diagnosed with a normal illness due to her being a god clone. Same with Kaworu. Evil Manga Kaworu was literally born yesterday to understand things. I don't think Shinji has autism or anything but he dose show very big signs of avoidance due to his self esteem issues. VERY big issues.

[]:             Quote Post by ILUVASUKALANGLEY » Mon Sep 09, 2013 11:51 am thanks I was wondering because I sometimes feel like Shinji at times since I have various things like Asperger's and defietly have self esteem issues so I've never been able to ask women out and when I tried I got traumatized, I am currently rereading the manga since I finally have volume 9 and volume 10 in three in one onimbus book. I missed those two volumes and 9 is still expensive. I've not watched the show since 2002 I think but have seen the rebuild ones. Did Shinji get traumatized with abandonment from his mother and did that make it hard to relate to females? the way I am they seem like a separate species sometimes and impossible to understand or even talk to. I think but would Misato and Asuka have abandonment issues as well? Like Misato and her dad so she uses sex to feel that she worth something. Asuka uses the Eva and school to prove she's worth something when her mother seem to obsessed with the doll instead of mothering Asuka.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Mon Sep 09, 2013 12:31 pm According to the November 1996 issue of Newtype, by way of Lawrence Eng: Do you wonder why Eva got so dark and psychological near the end? After all, Anno is the guy who directed Nadia of the Mysterious Seas, one of the liveliest and funniest anime I've ever watched. According to Anno, from episode 16 on, he began reading books about human psychology and became very interested. He wanted to explore "what the human mind is all about inside." "I wrote about myself. My friend lent me a book on psychological illness and this gave me a shock, as if I finally found what I needed to say," he says in the November Newtype.

[]:             Quote Post by ILUVASUKALANGLEY » Mon Sep 09, 2013 3:08 pm thank you for that link I don't recall ever seeing that but I knew that Anno was exploring his self as each aspect seem to represent a little of him.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Mon Sep 09, 2013 5:09 pm Even setting aside the fact that attempting to apply psychology to fictional is inevitably going to be an imperfect process to some degree (even if they are written by Anno), a factor that probably makes this "what personality disorders might the characters have" premise is that the characters in Eva are very multilayered creations and sometimes these layers merge or gradate into one another. I guess what i mean is that they were never intended to appear human, or at least it was never their primary goal. Also, i always had the impression that Anno had the intention of "exploring the human mind" since the start rather than some time around episode 16, but then again i'm rather hazy on the details of the story behind ep. 16's production.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Sep 10, 2013 7:50 am NemZ wrote: Some theorize that the children are modeled vaguelly on personality dissorers from different 'clusters'. Specifically, that Shinji is Avoidant, Asuka is Histrionic, and Rei is Schizotypal. Google those and decide for yourself if you think they fit.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Tue Sep 10, 2013 8:35 am Bagheera wrote: I believe the consensus was that Rei is Schizoid, not Schizotypal.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Sep 10, 2013 10:11 am UrsusArctos wrote: By the way, the "Schizoid" diagnosis has attracted significant controversy from psychiatrists who consider it equivalent to classifying introversion as a mental disorder. Of course, Rei is by any standard extreme .

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Tue Sep 10, 2013 12:08 pm Why do Asuka's synch problems require any explanation beyond her psychological decline?

[]:             Quote Post by Shinoyami65 » Tue Sep 10, 2013 12:15 pm Chuckman wrote: Why do Asuka's synch problems require any explanation beyond her psychological decline?

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Sep 10, 2013 12:37 pm Chuckman wrote: Why do Asuka's synch problems require any explanation beyond her psychological decline?

[]:             Quote Post by Rei IV » Tue Sep 10, 2013 12:58 pm You would think the task of saving the world from alien-like creatures, whose origins are not clear/unknown and have shown to be extremely dangerous/threatening, the kids would get some level of psychological help or at least some therapy for their mental health. Come to think of it, the whole cast would benefit from a nice, long therapy session.....but that would be inconvenient against the plot of Eva and NERV's interests. BrikHaus actually wrote something pertaining to the diagnosis of the children a while back, so I may have to look it up. All I remember was that Rei was the one most likely in "danger" if she did not get any sort of help.

[]:             Quote Post by ILUVASUKALANGLEY » Tue Sep 10, 2013 6:31 pm UrsusArctos wrote: By the way, the "Schizoid" diagnosis has attracted significant controversy from psychiatrists who consider it equivalent to classifying introversion as a mental disorder. Of course, Rei is by any standard extreme . While I don't have the source handy, I believe that Anno's direction to Megumi Hayashibara was to portray Rei as a girl who doesn't understand what emotions are, even if she does display them (rather prominently at times). Don't know what particular disorder that falls under, though.

[]:             Quote Post by SimplyMason0 » Tue Sep 10, 2013 6:34 pm Bagheera wrote: Rei is not a god clone, she's just a human soul in a clone's body. The SoLs have the same souls we do and are human just like anyone else. No, Rei's big issue is the fact that her soul is divided; as we see with Kyoko that tends to leave a mark.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Sep 11, 2013 10:30 am I always thought of assigning psychological diagnoses to fictional characters (even those who actually DO have a screw lose) as just plain lazy, because it means you can just assign a label and not bother with working through the layers upon layers on individual, interconnected reasons and inner mechanics and story elements that the authors created. Also, Autism gets thrown around a lot as internet joke term for regular social ineptitude, but it's by far nothing as simple as that - it's notably not an aquired, but an inherent thing (even through victims of very extreme neglect end up looking somewhat similar, but we're talking of feral-child levels of extreme), that can even be spotted by looking at the surface of the brain (a huge fold tends to be is different because the wiring is off) or be spotted in toddlers. It's notably not reversible, even if ppl can learn to work around it through concious effort. (Which disqualifies Rei, who eventually developed sufficiently spontaneous reactions. Shinji isn't even anywhere in the neighborhood. I mean, before a lot of things happened that would drive anyone nuts (having everyone you care about either die, leave or go nuts on you. Even most previously normal people would have a breaking point somewhere. ) he was pretty much having a normal school life with a bunch of friends and all. Consider that a bit of moodiness is quite common at that age. Most of those ppl spend three years or so lamenting the hopelesness of their lives in the youtube comment sections of depressing music, and then grow up and become normal, productive members of society - You'd find one or two of these in every average classroom. It's just that they don't usually get drafted into wars , at least not before all that is given some more time to resolve itself. He's certainly somewhat below average, but not particularly far outside the normal spectrum - he's actually relatively reasonable and nice to get along with as far as the moodier third of the earths teenagers is concerned. There's worse out there. ) As for Rei, consider that a) our only other example of a SoL in a human body seems very much capable of enjoyiing his existence, down to small things like a simple shower. And Rei should be significantly *closer* to a human than Kaworu is. Not to mention that even Kaworu could theoretically have been given a more normal upbringing that whatever SEELE gave him. b) When little Rei I is first introduced to us, she smiles at being shown smiling at being shown around the GEHIRN headquarters, and looks up to Ritsuko and Naoko when they greet her. We also see her perform the complete anoyying little sister act, teasing grin included. "Haha, daddy told me something you don't know~ Nenenene..." c) The entirity opisodes 15 to 19. Given a little imput, she acts pretty much like a normal, if somewhat inexperienced and quiet teenage girl, doing teenage girl things such as comming along with the rest of the group when they walzed off without waiting for orders, and looking visibly displeased when others touch her stuff. Even as she is, "split godess soul" or no, she is not incapable of positive experiences, nor does it take particularly much for her to have them, even if the circumstances of her life make them rare. Just because Rei has some circumstances that would justify her having "inherent" differences to anyone else, it doesn't mean that any and all differences observed in her must be of the inherent sort by default . Plus this whole lot of thematic subtext about angels and similar beings not being actually all that different from us, if it weren't for the impossibility of coexistence. She doesn't really display any less (usually even significantly more ) emotional reactions or aptitude at face-to-face communication than Gendo does, and "He's a human with a horrible backstory" is apparently a sufficient explanation for him. (But no, women are expected to be all pleasant and smiley, even without explicit reasons, right? Well, Rei doesn't know that. ) She grew up in a lab, with very limited human contact, and she always had to carry the hard burden of knowing that she is a) created for the sole sake of a specific purpose and b) could easily be replaced at any moment, without most ppl around her even noticing. This is not to say that the Lillith factor is nonexistent, but it's by far not all there is, or the all-encompassing and explaining. root that the situation can completely be reduced to. In either case, there IS this one prano interview where Anno mentions that a "shizoid disconnect thing" (See number-kuns tumblr. In the actual thing, he writes "shizophrenic", but he probably means "shizoid". The former is something very different, often simplified to that whole "hearing voices" thing. ) was one of the things he was trying to do with Rei, though one of the things that influenced her becoming the way she did may have been that he had a weird disconnect with writing her. We must remember that, all the crazy Scifi stuff aside, these are meant to be metaphors for the kinds of ppl this society produces. I once reas this essay on a German site, that compared Rei to children who spent long amounts of time either in hospitals, or with always changing teachers or babysitters, or programs meant to prepare them for a later school life (which, for the Japanese, can never start early enough), or otherwise recieving sufficient, but relatively "unpersonal" care. A normal toddler who didn't get the personal attention of a parent would just fail to develop and die, or, in lesser cases, become sort of apathic and disconnected, but Rei was already created as an apparent four-year old, and giving things a normal kid wouldn't pick up if they weren't correctly cared for (like speech) by artificial means, so she lives and functions, but lacks these experiences. Differentiating and expressing your feelings is kind of something you do learn in the first three years of your life. The "social smile" only appears at a month or so.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Sep 11, 2013 11:02 am Kendrix wrote: I always thought of assigning psychological diagnoses to fictional characters (even those who actually DO have a screw lose) as just plain lazy, because it means you can just assign a label and not bother with working through the layers upon layers on individual, interconnected reasons and inner mechanics and story elements that the authors created.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Sep 11, 2013 11:33 am Bagheera wrote: It's also not something we've claimed applies to the characters in the show.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Sep 11, 2013 12:14 pm Kendrix wrote: Uh, some earlier posters were .

Sorry if its been asked before I've not been on here
